title: CanopyER: Budgeted Rewrite-vs-Match Scheduling with Local Canonicalization and Incremental Blocker Maintenance
description: CanopyER frames progressive entity matching as a narrow budget-allocation problem: spend the next unit of work either on evaluating the current candidate frontier or on a blocker-local canonicalization that incrementally mutates the candidate graph. The contribution is a modest systems integration of progressive scheduling, local deterministic cleaning, and incremental blocker maintenance, not a new ER paradigm.
motivation: Fixed-representation progressive EM methods cannot spend budget to expose missing candidates, while iterative dirty-data and streaming ER systems mutate state for different reasons such as repair loops or new arrivals. The gap is a fixed-input, CPU-bounded setting where the scheduler must decide whether a local rewrite of existing records is worth more than another batch of comparisons right now.
proposed_approach: Use a greedy scheduler that scores `match` batches against canopy-local `clean` actions with a common gain-risk-cost view. Clean actions use deterministic operators, reproducible abbreviation and typo rules, and incremental blocker mutation so only touched postings and neighboring candidate regions are refreshed; the gain/risk model is deliberately lightweight, cross-fitted across dataset-settings, and evaluated mainly by whether it beats simple mutable-action baselines.

related_work.paper: Detective Gadget: Generic Iterative Entity Resolution over Dirty Data
related_work.authors: Marcello Buoncristiano, Giansalvatore Mecca, Donatello Santoro, and Enzo Veltri
related_work.difference: Closest conceptual neighbor, but optimized for iterative quality improvement on dirty data rather than budgeted competition between comparison batches and blocker-local rewrites.

related_work.authors: Steven Euijong Whang, David Menestrina, Georgia Koutrika, Martin Theobald, and Hector Garcia-Molina
related_work.difference: Mutates blocking structure through iterative evidence propagation, but does not schedule local canonicalization against pair evaluation under a time budget.

related_work.paper: ProgressER: Adaptive Progressive Approach to Relational Entity Resolution
related_work.authors: Yasser Altowim, Dmitri V. Kalashnikov, and Sharad Mehrotra
related_work.difference: A strong progressive ER scheduler over fixed record representations; it does not allow representation-rewrite actions that mutate the candidate graph during the run.

related_work.paper: Progressive Entity Resolution over Incremental Data
related_work.difference: Already studies progressive ER on a changing comparison space, but the graph changes because new data increments arrive. CanopyER instead assumes fixed input data and makes graph mutation an explicit budgeted action via local canonicalization.

related_work.paper: Incremental Entity Blocking over Heterogeneous Streaming Data
related_work.authors: Thais Batista Araujo, Kostas Stefanidis, Carlos Eduardo S. Pires, Jari Nummenmaa, and Tatiane Pereira da Nobrega
related_work.difference: Provides relevant incremental blocker maintenance for noisy streaming arrivals, but optimizes block updates rather than progressive duplicate yield and does not schedule blocker mutation against pair evaluation.

related_work.paper: Progressive Entity Matching via Cost Benefit Analysis
related_work.authors: Chenchen Sun, Zhijiang Hou, Derong Shen, and Tiezheng Nie
related_work.difference: Schedules static partitions by cost-benefit ratio. CanopyER instead schedules blocker-local rewrites online and incrementally updates only the touched candidate neighborhoods.

related_work.paper: Progressive Entity Matching: A Design Space Exploration
related_work.authors: Jakub Maciejewski, Konstantinos Nikoletos, George Papadakis, and Yannis Velegrakis
related_work.difference: Provides strong fixed-representation edge-centric, node-centric, and hybrid schedulers, which CanopyER treats as baselines before adding mutable-representation actions.

related_work.paper: Effective and Efficient Data Cleaning for Entity Matching
related_work.difference: Shows that targeted cleaning affects EM quality, but not inside a progressive scheduler that must price cleaning work against matching work in real time.

hypothesis: Under a fixed CPU budget on fixed-input dirty integration tasks, progressive entity matching will recover more duplicates earlier by explicitly scheduling blocker-local cleaning actions against comparison batches on a mutable candidate graph than by relying only on fixed-representation progressive schedulers, global upfront cleaning, or trivial mutable-action policies.
success_criteria: CanopyER beats RawPEM on progressive AUC in at least 4 of 5 dataset-settings.
CanopyER beats LocalHeuristic and MutableGreedy in at least 4 of 5 dataset-settings, showing that the lightweight estimator matters beyond localized cleaning alone.
CanopyER matches or exceeds HybridStatic on mean progressive AUC across the corrupted settings, demonstrating value beyond strong fixed-representation scheduling.
Incremental blocker mutation is at least 2x cheaper than FullReblock for the same selected clean actions.
Predicted clean-action gain is positively correlated with realized unlocked duplicates and does not induce large precision loss.
Failure analysis identifies concrete canopy or operator conditions where cleaning hurts precision or wastes budget.
